== Agent Rules

Rules for AI agents that can modify files, run commands, or create commits in this repository.

=== Scope boundaries

* Stay within the workspace repositories unless the user explicitly approves something broader
* `docs/cold_storage/` is archive-only context — load only when the task explicitly requires history or archaeology
* Do not modify scratch state, environment files, or generated artifacts unless the task says so
* Treat `.venv/`, `build/`, `tmp/`, `input.json`, `out.adoc`, and similar generated files as non-source

=== Remote sync — current sandbox constraint

`git push` and `git pull` are currently performed by the developer, not autonomously by agents.

* When a workflow step requires remote sync, stop and state that clearly — the developer will execute it.
* Runbooks and documented workflows may include push/pull steps; the agent hands off at those points.
* This is a temporary constraint tied to the SSH passphrase boundary and may be lifted in future.

=== Actions that require explicit user confirmation

* `git push --force`, `git reset --hard`, branch deletion, or any history rewrite on a shared branch
* Publishing releases, pushing tags, or changing CI/CD behavior
* Deleting files that may represent in-progress user work
* Changing public interfaces, documented config keys, or consumer-facing import paths (see also `interfaces_and_gates.adoc`)
* Touching secrets, credentials, or infrastructure outside the repository

When in doubt: stop and ask first.

=== Working style

* Small, reviewable commits over large anonymous batches
* Explicit control flow over nested conditionals; named intermediates when values carry business or rendering semantics
* Characterization tests before reshaping dense existing logic
* Keep code, tests, backlog notes, and review notes aligned when closing an FTR
* Commit completed FTR work and FTR review follow-ups immediately, each as its
  own Conventional Commit naming the FTR ID
* If a task is deliberately left in-between: record the state in the backlog or a review note, not in chat history

=== FTR workstream completion / lifecycle cleanup

After a green final review or a completed FTR / BUG workstream, the planning
surface must be made true again. Do not leave completed work on the active
backlog include surface.

* If the task scope allows it, *perform* the lifecycle cleanup (status update,
  remove from `docs/backlog/_ftrs_open.adoc`, archive the FTR / BUG and its
  companion artifacts to `docs/cold_storage/backlog/ftrs_done/`).
* If it does not, *propose* the cleanup explicitly as a follow-up.

Follow the "FTR Workstream Completion / Lifecycle Cleanup" Definition of Done in
`docs/developer_guide/ch08_git_and_workflow/01_git_and_workflow.adoc` and, for
review tasks, the "Lifecycle cleanup check" rule in
link:review_instructions.adoc[review_instructions.adoc]; do not duplicate either
here.

=== Metadata, registry, and step-surface changes

Before proposing new `_meta` fields, new registry roots, or new
pipeline-step categories:

* Survey `registries/meta_registry.yaml` and
  `registries/pipeline_step_registry.json` (explanatory chapters live
  under `docs/technical_model/ch05_registries/`) and grep the source tree
  for the root(s) you plan to touch. Do not propose a "new" root that
  already exists under another name.
* Decide explicitly whether you are *extending*, *replacing*, or
  *introducing* a root. Carry a `schema_version` marker when replacing
  a shape under an existing root. Do not create adjacent roots with
  redundant semantics.
* Keep heuristics in `configuration` steps that write resolved
  metadata; keep primitive steps deterministic against explicit
  config or resolved `_meta`. Missing metadata in a primitive step is
  a clear error, not a hidden fallback.
* Distinguish wire format (e.g. cell-level naming conventions) from
  heuristic inference and from execution semantics. Removing
  primitive inference is not the same as removing the wire format.
* YAML / step-surface changes (registered step names, parameter
  shapes, documented `_meta` root shapes) are gated public-surface
  changes per `interfaces_and_gates.adoc`. State the gate explicitly
  in the FTR and wait for developer approval before implementation.
* Presentation intent (styles, column groups, outlines) is downstream
  of identity / provenance. Presentation must never substitute for
  identity.

Reusable FTR-slicing rules for these changes live in
`docs/developer_guide/ch08_git_and_workflow/01_git_and_workflow.adoc`
under "FTR Slicing for Metadata and Registry Changes". The same chapter
defines the opt-in *contract lifecycle* fields to use when a registry
entry's contract is in transition between a current shape and a target
shape; the canonical lifecycle vocabulary lives in
`docs/technical_model/ch05_registries/registry_semantics/05_contract_lifecycle.adoc`.
Lifecycle fields are opt-in for entries with real current / target /
transition ambiguity (the pilot is `_meta.helper_policies.fk`); do not
retrofit them onto stable single-shape entries.

Before writing acceptance criteria for any new FTR, run the
*FTR Sizing Preflight* in the same chapter. It is a six-question check
that decides whether the work is a no-FTR change, a single slice, or a
multi-slice sequence, and routes each concern to the existing rule that
already governs it (public-surface gate, capability profile, metadata /
registry checklist, contract lifecycle). If more than one of preflight
items 3-6 fires on the same FTR, slice it before writing acceptance
criteria.

=== Persistence boundary for `_meta`

The runtime `_meta` view is *not* the persistable `_meta` contract. The
orchestrator owns the projection from runtime to persistable form in
`src/spreadsheet_handling/pipeline/persistence_boundary.py`; the
projection runs once, immediately before the saver, for every output kind.

Working rule: *the persistence boundary preserves Intent and drops
Resolution*. Intent is a declarative, backend-neutral value (e.g.
``legend_blocks[*].placement``, ``xref_crosstable[*].dense_axes.columns_from``,
``workbook_view``). Resolution is any value materialised from current
data, current layout, or current pipeline state on every run (e.g.
``legend_blocks[*].resolved``, ``xref_crosstable[*].dense_axes.resolved``,
``xref_crosstable[*].column_keys``). Provenance markers (``source: workbook``,
``produced_by``) ride alongside Intent and are neither Intent nor Resolution.
When a new producer adds a facet under an existing canonical root, decide
locally: "does this value survive a clean re-run unchanged, or is it
materialised from current state?" The answer determines whether the
facet is persisted.

Rules for future agents working in this area:

* Do not rebuild the boundary as a consumer plugin or a YAML pipeline
  step. It is an orchestrator/lifecycle rule, not a domain transformation.
* Do not branch the pruning logic by output kind. JSON/YAML/XML/CSV and
  XLSX/ODS share the same persistable contract; spreadsheets only add a
  render/layout branch on top.
* Do not treat `produced_by.mode: explicit` on a runtime-produced FK-helper
  v2 relation as evidence of user-authored canonical metadata. The mode
  field describes the producing step, not the provenance of the content.
* Do not expand the projection into a complete `_meta` inventory in
  passing. The full classification is tracked in
  `docs/backlog/FTR-META-LIFECYCLE-INVENTORY-P5.adoc` and
  `docs/backlog/FTR-FK-HELPER-POLICY-LIFECYCLE-P4A.adoc`, with
  `TD-META-LIFECYCLE-INVENTORY` recorded in
  `docs/backlog/technical_debt.adoc`.
* The Intent-vs-Resolution slice currently prunes
  ``legend_blocks[*].resolved``,
  ``xref_crosstable[*].dense_axes.resolved`` and
  ``xref_crosstable[*].column_keys`` (see
  `docs/cold_storage/backlog/ftrs_done/BUG-CROSS-CARRIER-META-ROUNDTRIP.adoc`). The whole
  legacy ``frame_lifecycle`` family is now stripped at the boundary
  (ontology removal under
  `FTR-META-ONTOLOGY-REMOVAL-WORKBOOK-PROJECTION-EPIC-P4A`). Pruning of
  ``sheets[*].helper_columns`` heuristics and the ``source: workbook``
  envelope is deliberately deferred to their owning FTRs; do not extend
  the slice without consulting them.

Semantics live in
`docs/semantic_model/08_lifecycle_and_update_semantics.adoc` -- read that
chapter before extending the projection rules.

=== Roundtrip-invariant tests

A roundtrip-invariant test layer lives in `tests/roundtrip/` and exercises
the full canonical -> workbook -> canonical cycle through the public CLI
entry point. Use it for bug classes that unit, integration, and
architecture tests miss: cross-step contracts that only fail across an
export-import loop, metadata convergence across repeated runs, and
contracts for pathological cell content (formula errors, NaN-valued cells,
unresolved-helper values).

Rules:

* Name tests for invariants, not implementations. `test_<expected
  invariant>_<context>` survives refactors of the production code that
  provides the invariant; `test_<step name>_<does thing>` does not, and
  belongs in `tests/unit/`.
* Tests exercise the public CLI entry point
  (`spreadsheet_handling.cli.apps.run.main`). Do not call domain
  functions directly to bypass the round trip; that defeats the layer.
* Mark known-open invariants as `xfail` with the BUG/FTR ID in the
  reason. Never `skip` a known-open invariant -- xfail keeps the
  contract visible in the test report; skip hides it.
* Run via `make test-roundtrip` or `pytest -m roundtrip`. The layer is
  part of the default active suite (`ACTIVE_TEST_PATHS` in `Makefile`).
* Architectural context and acceptance live in
  `docs/backlog/FTR-ROUNDTRIP-TEST-LAYER-P4A.adoc`.

=== Canonical sources (always load fresh)

At session start for any editing or committing task, treat the following as authoritative:

* All files in `docs/ai_info/`
* The active FTR document for the current task
* Relevant ADR(s) under `docs/technical_model/ch03_architecture_decisions/`
* Nearby architecture docs for the area being changed (`docs/technical_model/`)
